lens.included_activities: Private automobile driving schools delivering classroom or online theory instruction, behind-the-wheel lessons, refresher training, and related noncommercial road-test preparation to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Public-school captive programs, truck and bus driving schools, stand-alone licensing agencies, software-only course publishers without an accountable school operator, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily consumer-paid fixed-price course and lesson packages, supplemented by hourly lessons and occasional institutional or publicly supported cohorts; each customer engagement is finite, while the school repeatedly delivers a standardized service to successive novice and refresher students.

a.rationale: Driving instructors are the defining occupation: O*NET lists individual and group instruction, observation of students, injury prevention, parent communication, and record maintenance among self-enrichment-teacher tasks and explicitly includes Driving Instructor as a reported title. AI can substitute for theory-content delivery, learning-material creation, routine student communications, scheduling, recordkeeping, and some assessment, but not most supervised in-car instruction. The range reflects a task-level wage-share judgment rather than a measured 611692 staffing matrix.
a.change_evidence: A current 611692-specific OEWS staffing table plus payroll-weighted time studies from multi-location driving-school operators separating classroom, in-car, scheduling, sales, and compliance work would materially change the estimate.
caveats: O*NET 25-3021 covers many self-enrichment teachers, not only driving instructors.
No national six-digit wage-weighted occupation mix or task-time study was located.
Exposure includes avoidable hiring as well as direct substitution and does not assume safe unsupervised vehicle instruction.

rho.rationale: Online delivery is already permitted for the 30-hour California driver-education component, and AAA already sells online education bundled with in-car lessons, so digital workflow adoption has a practical base. Implementation is constrained by state approvals, curriculum and certificate controls, learner safety, and the need to integrate theory with observed behind-the-wheel performance. The estimate applies only to the exposed opportunity in a five-year operating program.
rho.change_evidence: Operator-level pilots measuring instructor hours, administrative hours, completion rates, incident rates, regulatory exceptions, and customer satisfaction before and after AI-assisted scheduling, tutoring, documentation, and lesson planning would materially move this range.
caveats: State rules vary materially and can slow or prohibit automated curriculum changes.
Observed availability of online coursework is not evidence that generative-AI workflows meet each state's approval, privacy, or record-retention requirements.
The estimate excludes pricing and demand effects.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition is unusually coherent and limited to automobile driving instruction, while truck and bus instruction is assigned elsewhere. Most private firms in the supplied LMM count should therefore operate transferable schools serving external customers. The haircut allows for owner-dependent practices, weakly documented cash businesses, schools reliant on a single instructor or license, and entities whose operations would not survive a control transfer.
e.change_evidence: A complete 611692 firm census matched to state school-license records, revenue mix, instructor headcount, owner hours, customer concentration, and transferable licenses would determine eligibility directly.
caveats: The supplied firm count is an estimate based on margin-bridged size classes rather than observed EBITDA.
Licenses, instructor certifications, and school approvals are state-specific and may not transfer automatically.
Eligibility is inferred from the industry definition, not observed across the 16 supplied firms.

s5.rationale: Small driving schools are commonly owner-operated, making retirement, burnout, and succession plausible transfer catalysts, but many exits will be closures or asset sales rather than qualifying control transfers. The five-year probability is therefore bounded below a simple owner-exit rate. Census ABS publishes owner-age characteristics for employer firms, but the available evidence is broader than 611692 and does not measure transfers.
s5.change_evidence: State license ownership-change files linked to five years of Secretary of State control changes, broker listings, SBA 7(a) acquisitions, and interviews with driving-school owners aged 55 and over would distinguish transfers from closures.
caveats: No national 611692 transaction-rate series was found.
Broad educational-services owner demographics do not reveal whether a school has a transferable management layer.
Deaths, internal reorganizations, and non-going-concern asset sales are excluded.

q.rationale: Consumer driving instruction is sold as posted fixed-price packages and hourly lessons rather than cost-plus reimbursement. AAA's California offer displayed $494 for online education plus six in-car hours and $589.05 for ten in-car hours, illustrating transparent package pricing. An operator can initially retain workflow savings, but low switching costs, visible local prices, renewal-free customer relationships, and competition from online theory courses should pass some benefit to customers over five years.
q.change_evidence: Cohort-level price, discount, lead-conversion, instructor-hour, and gross-margin data from several local markets following measurable workflow savings would show the actual repricing and competitive pass-through path.
caveats: One AAA regional offer is not a national price sample.
Many customers buy only once, so retention occurs through posted-price discipline and new-customer competition rather than contract renewal.
The estimate excludes demand loss and implementation difficulty.

d5.bridge: FHWA measures licensed drivers, not paid driving-school instruction. Total drivers aged 19 and under increased from 8.782 million in 2019 to 9.083 million in 2024, while total licensed drivers increased from 228.680 million to 239.825 million; BLS projects the broader self-enrichment-teacher occupation to grow 3.7% from 2024 to 2034. I translate the flat-to-low-single-digit observed/projection signals into a five-year constant-price service-quantity ratio centered at 1.02. This likely overstates school demand if home supervision, direct DMV testing, or online-only substitutes gain share, and understates it if state mandates, delayed licensure, adult immigration, or refresher training rise.
d5.rationale: The novice-driver pipeline has been stable to modestly growing rather than collapsing, and the broader occupation has a small positive ten-year employment projection. Because licensure counts are stocks and paid lessons are flows, a wide range allows for state rules, cohort timing, adult learners, and post-pandemic normalization.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of 611692 constant-price receipts or course completions by student age, combined with Census national age projections and state driver-education mandate changes, would replace the licensed-driver and occupation proxies.
caveats: Licensed-driver stocks include people who never used a commercial school and omit lesson intensity.
The 2019-to-2024 comparison spans pandemic disruption.
Self-enrichment teachers include many activities unrelated to driving instruction.

o.rationale: Classroom theory can be self-served online, but a large share of the current service basket is supervised vehicle practice, safety assessment, progress feedback, certification, and state-accountable recordkeeping. California permits home-study or internet theory yet still requires minors to complete at least six hours behind the wheel with a professional instructor; NHTSA describes 30 classroom and six behind-the-wheel hours as the most common model nationally while emphasizing state variation. Software should reduce operator labor without eliminating the licensed school's accountable role in most practical instruction over five years.
o.change_evidence: A state-by-state revenue-weighted map of mandatory professional hours, online-course permissions, third-party testing rules, enrollment mix, and approved remote or simulator substitutes would quantify the operator-required share.
caveats: California is a large but not representative national rule set.
NHTSA's common-hours statement does not establish the revenue share of classroom versus in-car delivery.
Advanced simulators or future regulatory changes could substitute for more practical instruction than assumed.

narrative.executive_view: Automobile driving schools combine a standardized, digitizable theory and administration layer with a safety-critical, locally delivered in-car core. The opportunity is therefore operational rather than fully virtual: improve scheduling, communications, curriculum support, recordkeeping, and instructor utilization while preserving accountable human supervision.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can draft and localize learning material, answer routine student questions, personalize theory practice, summarize instructor notes, schedule cars and instructors, flag incomplete records, and support sales follow-up. It is much less suited to taking responsibility for a novice in live traffic, judging situational awareness, or certifying practical competence without an instructor and approved process.
narrative.value_capture: Posted package and hourly pricing allow savings to be retained before local competitors reprice. Transparent consumer comparison, one-time customers, and substitutable online theory courses create meaningful medium-term pass-through, while scarce instructor and vehicle capacity can let a well-run operator retain savings through faster throughput and availability.
narrative.firm_availability: The industry definition is coherent, but the supplied LMM population is only 16 firms and some apparent candidates may be inseparable from a working owner or state-specific license. A real pipeline depends more on transferable instructor rosters, compliant records, vehicles, and local reputation than on a corporate shell.
narrative.demand_durability: Teen licensure remains a durable replenishing need, augmented by adult beginners, immigrants, refresher students, and test preparation. Online theory can displace classroom hours, but current state systems continue to attach value to supervised practice, completion records, and professional certification. Five-year quantity is more likely stable than structurally high-growth.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main risks are state-by-state regulation, instructor scarcity, insurance and vehicle costs, safety liability, customer acquisition dependence, and the possibility that AI-enabled online courses commoditize the easiest work. Evidence is weakest on six-digit occupational mix, owner succession, actual control-transfer rates, and national lesson volumes.
narrative.principal_driver: A repeatable digital administration and theory layer sits beside a regulated, capacity-constrained in-car service that still needs an accountable local operator.
narrative.principal_weakness: The LMM firm pool is small, and much of the industry's payroll remains tied to live behind-the-wheel instruction that AI cannot safely remove.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: Sector 61, NAICS 611692 Automobile Driving Schools
sources.supports: Defines 611692 as establishments primarily offering automobile driving instruction and assigns truck and bus driving instruction to 611519.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Self-Enrichment Teachers (25-3021.00)
sources.supports: Lists Driving Instructor as a reported title and identifies instruction, adaptation, student observation, injury prevention, parent communication, and record maintenance among the occupation's tasks.

sources.title: California DMV: Driver Training Schools
sources.supports: States that under-18 students must complete classroom driver education, permits home-study or internet delivery for the 30-hour theory requirement, and requires at least six hours of behind-the-wheel training with a professional instructor.

sources.title: NHTSA: Pre-Licensure Driver Education
sources.supports: Reports that driver education most commonly includes 30 classroom hours and six behind-the-wheel hours, notes material variation across states, and discusses state incentives and safety-evidence limitations.

sources.title: AAA Auto Club Driving School: Online and Behind-the-Wheel Teen Driver Training
sources.supports: Displays a $494 package for online education plus six in-car hours and a $589.05 package for online education plus ten in-car hours, illustrating bundled fixed-fee consumer pricing and existing online delivery.

sources.title: FHWA Highway Statistics 2024, Table DL-220: Licensed Drivers by Sex and Age Group, 1963-2024
sources.supports: Reports 8.782 million licensed drivers aged 19 and under and 228.680 million total licensed drivers in 2019 versus 9.083 million and 239.825 million, respectively, in 2024.

sources.title: BLS: Occupational Projections and Worker Characteristics, 2024-2034
sources.supports: Projects self-enrichment-teacher employment from 417,500 in 2024 to 433,000 in 2034, a 3.7% increase; this occupation includes driving instructors but is much broader.

sources.title: Census Bureau: 2023 Annual Business Survey Characteristics of Business Owners
sources.supports: Confirms that the 2023 ABS covers reference year 2022 and publishes Age of Owner characteristics for employer businesses; it does not measure 611692 control transfers.
